histogram: A graphical representation showing the distribution of numerical data by depicting the frequencies of data points within specified intervals or 'bins'.
outlier: A data point that differs significantly from other observations. Datagrad detects outliers using IQR-based and standard-deviation-based methods.
continuous variable: A numeric variable that can take on an infinite number of values between any two given values.
categorical variable: A variable containing a finite number of categories or distinct groups. It can be string or number. The data might not have a logical order.
ordinal variable: A categorical variable whose categories have a meaningful, ranked order (e.g. low → medium → high), but the intervals between ranks are not necessarily equal.
binary variable: A variable that takes only two possible values, such as 0/1, yes/no, or true/false.
outcome variable: The variable you are trying to explain or predict in an analysis. Also called the dependent variable or response variable.
predictor: A variable used to explain or predict changes in the outcome variable. Also called an independent variable or explanatory variable.
p-value: The probability of obtaining a test result at least as extreme as the one observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (typically < 0.05) suggests the result is unlikely under the null hypothesis.
null hypothesis: The default assumption in a statistical test, typically stating that there is no effect or no difference between groups.
alternative hypothesis: The hypothesis that contradicts the null hypothesis, proposing that an effect or difference does exist.
significance level: The threshold (commonly 0.05) below which a p-value is considered statistically significant, leading to rejection of the null hypothesis.
confidence interval: A range of values, calculated from sample data, that is likely to contain the true population parameter. A 95% confidence interval means that if the study were repeated many times, about 95% of such intervals would contain the true value.
effect size: A quantitative measure of the magnitude of a phenomenon, independent of sample size. Examples include Cohen's d, eta-squared, and Cramér's V.
normal distribution: A symmetric, bell-shaped probability distribution defined by its mean and standard deviation. Many statistical tests assume the data follow this distribution.
normality: The property of data being approximately normally distributed. Several tests in Datagrad check this assumption before choosing parametric vs. non-parametric methods.
parametric test: A statistical test that makes assumptions about the distribution of the data (typically normality). Examples include the t-test and ANOVA.
non-parametric test: A statistical test that does not assume a specific distribution for the data. Examples include the Mann-Whitney U test and the Kruskal-Wallis test.
mean: The arithmetic average of a set of values, calculated by summing all values and dividing by the count.
median: The middle value in a sorted dataset. It divides the data into two equal halves and is less affected by outliers than the mean.
mode: The most frequently occurring value in a dataset.
standard deviation: A measure of the amount of variation or spread in a set of values. A low standard deviation indicates data points are close to the mean.
variance: The square of the standard deviation. It measures how far each data point is from the mean, on average.
IQR: Interquartile Range — the difference between the 75th percentile (Q3) and the 25th percentile (Q1). It measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data.
skewness: A measure of the asymmetry of a distribution. Positive skew means the right tail is longer; negative skew means the left tail is longer.
kurtosis: A measure of how heavy or light the tails of a distribution are compared to a normal distribution. High kurtosis indicates heavy tails and a sharp peak.
correlation: A statistical measure that describes the strength and direction of a linear (or monotonic) relationship between two variables.
correlation coefficient: A numerical value between -1 and +1 that quantifies the strength and direction of a relationship between two variables.
scatter plot: A chart that uses dots to represent the values of two different variables, useful for identifying relationships, clusters, and outliers.
box plot: A standardized way of displaying the distribution of data based on a five-number summary: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum. Outliers are shown as individual points.
violin plot: A combination of a box plot and a density plot, showing the full distribution shape of the data alongside summary statistics.
bar chart: A chart that presents categorical data using rectangular bars whose heights or lengths are proportional to the values they represent.
line chart: A chart that displays data points connected by straight line segments, often used to show trends over time.
density plot: A smoothed, continuous version of a histogram that estimates the probability density function of a variable.
QQ plot: Quantile-Quantile plot — a graphical tool to assess whether data follow a particular theoretical distribution (typically the normal distribution) by plotting observed quantiles against theoretical quantiles.
heatmap: A graphical representation of data where individual values in a matrix are represented by colors, commonly used to display correlation matrices.
pie chart: A circular chart divided into slices to illustrate the proportional sizes of categories within a dataset.
Shapiro-Wilk test: A normality test that evaluates whether a sample comes from a normally distributed population. It is considered one of the most powerful normality tests for small to moderate sample sizes.
Lilliefors test: A modification of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test specifically designed to test for normality when the mean and variance are estimated from the data.
Pearson correlation: A measure of the linear relationship between two continuous variables. Values range from -1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive).
Spearman correlation: A non-parametric measure of the monotonic relationship between two variables, based on ranks rather than raw values.
Kendall's tau: A non-parametric measure of the ordinal association between two variables, based on the number of concordant and discordant pairs.
distance correlation: A measure of dependence between two variables that can detect both linear and non-linear associations. Unlike Pearson, a distance correlation of zero implies statistical independence.
point-biserial correlation: A correlation measure between a naturally binary variable and a continuous variable. It is mathematically equivalent to the Pearson correlation in this special case.
rank-biserial correlation: An effect size measure associated with the Mann-Whitney U test, quantifying the degree to which values in one group tend to be larger than values in the other.
Cramér's V: A measure of association between two categorical variables, based on the chi-squared statistic. Values range from 0 (no association) to 1 (perfect association).
eta-squared: A measure of effect size for the relationship between a categorical variable and a continuous variable. It represents the proportion of variance in the continuous variable explained by group membership.
mutual information: An information-theoretic measure of the dependence between two variables. It captures any kind of relationship (linear or non-linear) and is measured in bits or nats.
partial correlation: The correlation between two variables after removing the linear effect of one or more other variables (covariates).
smart correlation: A Datagrad feature that automatically selects the most appropriate correlation method for each pair of variables based on their data types and distributions.
t-test: A statistical test used to compare the means of two groups. There are two main variants: the independent t-test (separate groups) and the paired t-test (same subjects measured twice).
independent t-test: A parametric test comparing the means of two unrelated groups, assuming the data are approximately normally distributed.
paired t-test: A parametric test comparing the means of two related measurements from the same subjects (e.g. before and after treatment).
Mann-Whitney U test: A non-parametric test comparing the distributions of two independent groups. It is used as an alternative to the independent t-test when normality cannot be assumed.
Wilcoxon signed-rank test: A non-parametric test comparing two related samples or repeated measurements. It is the non-parametric alternative to the paired t-test.
ANOVA: Analysis of Variance — a parametric test comparing the means of three or more groups. It tests whether at least one group mean differs significantly from the others.
Welch's ANOVA: A modification of one-way ANOVA that does not assume equal variances across groups, making it more robust to heteroscedasticity.
Kruskal-Wallis test: A non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA that compares the distributions of three or more independent groups using ranks.
post-hoc test: A statistical test performed after an omnibus test (like ANOVA) finds a significant result, to determine which specific groups differ from each other.
Tukey's HSD: Tukey's Honestly Significant Difference — a post-hoc test used after ANOVA to make pairwise comparisons between all group means while controlling the family-wise error rate.
Dunn's test: A non-parametric post-hoc test used after a Kruskal-Wallis test to identify which specific pairs of groups differ.
chi-squared test: A test of association between two categorical variables, based on comparing observed frequencies to those expected under independence.
Fisher's exact test: A test of association between two categorical variables that is exact (rather than approximate) and is especially useful when sample sizes are small or expected cell counts are low.
McNemar's test: A test used on paired nominal data to determine whether there is a significant change in proportions across two conditions (e.g. before vs. after).
Levene's test: A test of equality of variances across groups. It is used to check the homogeneity of variance assumption required by tests like ANOVA.
homogeneity of variance: The assumption that different groups in a comparison have equal variances. Violated homogeneity may require using Welch's ANOVA or non-parametric alternatives.
MANOVA: Multivariate Analysis of Variance — an extension of ANOVA that tests for group differences across two or more continuous outcome variables simultaneously.
Pillai's trace: A test statistic used in MANOVA that is considered the most robust to violations of assumptions. Values closer to 1 indicate stronger group separation.
group separation: A Datagrad analysis that evaluates how well subsets of predictor variables can distinguish between groups. It ranks variable combinations by a separation score and cross-validated accuracy.
classification analysis: A Datagrad analysis that evaluates individual predictors as diagnostic classifiers for a binary outcome, producing ROC curves for numeric predictors and confusion matrices for binary predictors.
ROC curve: Receiver Operating Characteristic curve — a plot of sensitivity vs. (1 − specificity) across all possible thresholds for a numeric predictor. A higher AUC indicates better discrimination.
AUC: Area Under the ROC Curve — a single number summarizing a predictor's ability to distinguish between two classes. AUC ranges from 0.5 (no better than chance) to 1.0 (perfect discrimination).
confusion matrix: A table showing the counts of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for a binary classifier.
sensitivity: The proportion of true positives correctly identified (also called recall or true positive rate). Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN).
specificity: The proportion of true negatives correctly identified. Specificity = TN / (TN + FP).
precision: The proportion of predicted positives that are actually positive. Precision = TP / (TP + FP).
F-score: The harmonic mean of precision and sensitivity, providing a single metric that balances both.
Youden's J: A statistic that identifies the optimal cutpoint on an ROC curve by maximizing (Sensitivity + Specificity − 1).
linear regression: A modeling technique that predicts a continuous outcome from one or more predictors by fitting a straight line (or hyperplane) that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
OLS: Ordinary Least Squares — the standard estimation method for linear regression, which minimizes the sum of squared differences between observed and predicted values.
logistic regression: A modeling technique for predicting a binary outcome. It estimates the probability of the outcome using the logistic (sigmoid) function.
mixed-effects model: A regression model that includes both fixed effects (standard predictors) and random effects (grouping variables that account for non-independence in the data, such as subjects or sites).
fixed effect: A predictor in a mixed model whose effect is estimated as a single value across the entire dataset.
random effect: A grouping variable in a mixed model that allows intercepts (and optionally slopes) to vary across levels, accounting for hierarchical or clustered data structures.
Cox proportional hazards: A survival analysis model that relates the time to an event (e.g. death, failure) to one or more predictor variables, without assuming a specific distribution for survival times.
survival analysis: A family of statistical methods for analyzing the expected duration of time until an event occurs (e.g. death, relapse, equipment failure).
hazard ratio: In a Cox model, the ratio of the hazard rates for a one-unit increase in a predictor. A hazard ratio > 1 indicates increased risk; < 1 indicates decreased risk.
Firth penalized regression: A bias-reduction technique for logistic and Cox regression models that is especially useful with small samples, rare events, or quasi-complete separation.
MLE: Maximum Likelihood Estimation — the standard method for estimating model parameters by finding the values that make the observed data most probable.
interaction term: A term in a regression model representing the combined effect of two or more predictors. It tests whether the relationship between a predictor and the outcome changes depending on the value of another predictor.
stepwise selection: An automated procedure for choosing which predictors to include in a regression model. Datagrad supports forward and backward stepwise selection based on Wald p-value, AIC, or BIC.
AIC: Akaike Information Criterion — a model comparison metric that balances goodness-of-fit with model complexity. Lower AIC indicates a better-fitting, more parsimonious model.
BIC: Bayesian Information Criterion — similar to AIC but with a stronger penalty for model complexity. Lower BIC is preferred.
R-squared: The proportion of variance in the outcome variable explained by the model. Values range from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating a better fit.
adjusted R-squared: A modified version of R-squared that penalizes for the number of predictors in the model, preventing overfitting from inflating the value.
collinearity: A condition where two or more predictor variables are highly correlated with each other, making it difficult to isolate their individual effects.
VIF: Variance Inflation Factor — a measure of how much the variance of a regression coefficient is inflated due to collinearity with other predictors. A VIF above 10 is commonly considered problematic.
target leakage: A situation where a predictor variable contains information that would not be available at the time of prediction, leading to artificially high model performance.
imputation: The process of replacing missing values in a dataset with estimated values. Datagrad's smart imputation uses median for numeric columns and mode for categorical columns.
missing value: A data point that is absent or not recorded. In Datagrad, missing values are shown as empty cells and are handled during imputation or excluded from analyses.
encoding: The process of converting categorical variables into numerical representations suitable for statistical analysis. Datagrad supports one-hot, dummy, ordinal, cumulative, and frequency encoding.
one-hot encoding: A method that creates a new binary column for each category in a variable. Each row gets a 1 in the column corresponding to its category and 0 elsewhere.
dummy encoding: Similar to one-hot encoding, but drops one category (the reference level) to avoid perfect collinearity. This is the standard approach for regression models.
ordinal encoding: A method that assigns integer values to categories based on a specified order (e.g. low=0, medium=1, high=2).
cumulative encoding: A method that creates binary columns representing cumulative thresholds along an ordered scale (e.g. 'at least medium', 'at least high').
frequency encoding: A method that replaces each category with its relative frequency (proportion) in the dataset.
recoding: The process of reassigning values within a variable, for example combining multiple categories into fewer groups or correcting data entry errors.
column correction: A Datagrad data wrangling feature that automatically detects and suggests fixes for typos, whitespace issues, near-number strings, and rare/singleton categories using SymSpell-based logic.
SymSpell: A fast spelling correction algorithm used by Datagrad's column correction feature to detect and fix typographical errors in categorical data.
type casting: Changing the data type of a column (e.g. from text to number, or number to category) to ensure it is treated correctly in analyses.
row exclusion: A Datagrad filtering mechanism that marks certain rows as excluded from analyses without permanently deleting them. Exclusions are based on column value rules.
descriptive statistics: Numerical summaries that describe the main features of a dataset, including measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode), spread (standard deviation, IQR), and shape (skewness, kurtosis).
numerical QC: Numerical Quality Control — a Datagrad report that examines each numeric column for outliers (IQR and standard deviation methods) and constant increment patterns.
constant increment pattern: A sequence of values in a column that increases or decreases by a fixed step, which may indicate an index or row-number artifact rather than genuine data.
grouped summary: Descriptive statistics calculated separately for each level of a grouping variable, allowing comparison of distributions across categories.
outcome analysis: A Datagrad smart analysis that automatically runs the most appropriate statistical test between every predictor and a set of specified outcome variables, based on variable types and distributions.
group analysis: A Datagrad smart analysis that automatically runs the most appropriate statistical test between every numeric variable and a set of specified grouping variables.
smart regression: A Datagrad feature that automatically selects the appropriate regression model type (OLS, Logistic, Mixed, or Cox) based on the outcome variable's characteristics and user-specified options.
interpretation: A Datagrad feature that translates raw statistical results (test statistics, p-values, effect sizes) into plain-language explanations.
Handsontable: The JavaScript spreadsheet library used by Datagrad's data grid, providing Excel-like editing, sorting, and formatting capabilities.
Plotly: The JavaScript/Python charting library used by Datagrad for interactive, exportable visualizations.
XLSX: The Excel file format (.xlsx) used by Datagrad for exporting cleaned data and multi-tabbed analysis reports.
SPSS file: A data file in IBM SPSS format (.sav). Datagrad can import SPSS files, including their variable labels and metadata.
CSV: Comma-Separated Values — a plain text file format for tabular data. Datagrad supports CSV import and export.
marginal plot: A secondary chart drawn along the edge of a scatter plot (e.g. a histogram, box plot, or violin plot) showing the distribution of each axis variable individually.
balanced accuracy: The average of sensitivity and specificity, providing a single performance metric that is not distorted by class imbalance.
macro F1: The unweighted average of the F-score across all classes, treating each class equally regardless of its frequency.
Fisher ratio: A group separation metric defined as the ratio of between-group variance to within-group variance. Higher values indicate clearer separation.
cross-validation: A resampling method that splits the data into multiple folds, training on some and testing on others, to estimate how well a model generalizes to unseen data.
